sets.clue: Guilty of faithlessness or disloyalty; deceitfully treacherous
sets.definition: Guilty of faithlessness or disloyalty; deceitfully treacherous.
sets.example: The perfidious advisor leaked the queen's plans to her enemies.

sets.definition: A person who behaves badly or in a way that breaks the law; a villain.
sets.example: The magistrate sentenced the miscreant to three months in the gaol.

sets.definition: Outstandingly bad; shockingly conspicuous.
sets.etymology: Latin e- (out of) + grex (flock, herd) + -ious — originally 'standing out from the flock' (in a good sense; later ironic)
sets.example: The report detailed an egregious failure of oversight at every level.

sets.clue: The _____ comparison drew complaints from workers who felt singled out.
sets.definition: Likely to arouse resentment because of perceived unfairness or discrimination.
sets.example: The invidious comparison drew complaints from workers who felt singled out.

sets.clue: Not honest; tending to mislead or trick.
sets.definition: Not honest; tending to mislead or trick.
sets.example: The untruthful witness changed her story three times.

sets.definition: Giving a wrong idea or false impression.
sets.example: The label was misleading — the cereal had far more sugar than it claimed.

sets.definition: Not able to be trusted or depended on.
sets.example: The old car was too unreliable for long trips.

sets.clue: The thief gave a ____ excuse the guard immediately doubted.
sets.definition: Not telling the truth; willing to lie or cheat.
sets.example: The thief gave a dishonest excuse the guard immediately doubted.

sets.clue: Acting against one's faith or loyalty; betraying trust.
sets.definition: The act of being disloyal to a person, country, or cause.
sets.example: His betrayal of the team's strategy cost them the match.

sets.definition: Deceitful; saying one thing while meaning another.
sets.example: The duplicitous diplomat agreed in public and undermined the treaty in private.

sets.definition: Without moral principles; willing to do wrong for gain.
sets.example: The unscrupulous banker hid losses from his clients for years.

sets.clue: The ____ spy passed his country's secrets to a foreign agent.
sets.definition: Behaving as a traitor; disloyal to one's country or cause.
sets.example: The traitorous spy passed his country's secrets to a foreign agent.

sets.definition: Having developed certain abilities or inclinations at an earlier age than is usual.
sets.etymology: Latin prae- (before) + coquere (to cook, to ripen) + -ious — 'ripened before its time'
sets.example: The precocious child was reading chapter books by the age of four.
sets.synonyms: advanced, gifted, ahead of one's time, prodigious

sets.clue: Feeling or showing deep regret or guilt for wrongdoing
sets.definition: Filled with deep regret or guilt for a wrong committed.
sets.example: He was genuinely remorseful and wrote letters of apology to all concerned.

sets.definition: Not intimidated or discouraged by difficulty, danger, or disappointment.
sets.etymology: Old English un- (not) + Old French danter (to tame, subdue) + -ed
sets.example: She remained undaunted by the scale of the task before her.

sets.definition: To regard or represent as being of little worth; to speak slightingly of.
sets.etymology: Old French des- (away from) + parage (rank, equality of birth) + -age — 'to lower in rank'
sets.example: He was careful never to disparage his rivals in public.

sets.clue: Having no thought; acting without considering consequences.
sets.definition: Having no thought; acting without considering consequences.
sets.example: His unthinking remark hurt her feelings before he realized what he had said.

sets.definition: A person who indulges in idle or pleasant fantasies.
sets.example: The teacher called on the daydreamer by the window, who hadn't heard a word.

sets.definition: The quality of paying full attention to the present moment.
sets.example: Her mindfulness in handling the fragile heirloom impressed her grandmother.

sets.clue: The ____ investor refused to invest without proof of every claim.
sets.definition: Unable or unwilling to trust; suspicious.
sets.example: The distrustful investor refused to invest without proof of every claim.

sets.clue: The act of examining one's own thoughts and feelings.
sets.definition: The act of examining one's own thoughts and feelings.
sets.example: After the failed exam, he spent the evening in quiet introspection.

sets.definition: Unwilling and hesitant; disinclined to act.
sets.example: She gave a reluctant nod when asked to chair the committee.

sets.definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action.
sets.example: The obstinate child folded her arms and refused to eat a single vegetable.

sets.clue: Her ____ to volunteer for hard tasks impressed her supervisor.
sets.definition: Firmness of purpose; resolute decision-making.
sets.example: Her determination to volunteer for hard tasks impressed her supervisor.

sets.clue: Difficult to control or deal with; stubbornly resistant to authority
sets.definition: Difficult to control or deal with; stubbornly resistant to persuasion or authority.
sets.etymology: Latin in- (not) + trahere (to draw, pull) + -able — 'unable to be drawn along'
sets.example: The intractable dispute had resisted every attempt at mediation.

sets.clue: Her _____ behavior during the meeting shocked everyone present.
sets.definition: Noisy and difficult to control; aggressively defiant.
sets.example: Her obstreperous behavior during the meeting shocked everyone present.

sets.definition: To make clear; to explain or clarify.
sets.etymology: Latin e- (out) + lucidus (clear, bright) + -ate — 'to bring out into the light'
sets.example: The professor paused to elucidate the distinction between the two theories.

sets.definition: To refuse to accept; to reject the authority or validity of.
sets.example: She was quick to repudiate the claims made on her behalf.

sets.clue: The act of making something stronger or firmer.
sets.definition: The act or process of making something stronger or more firm.
sets.example: The bridge needed strengthening before the heavy trucks could cross.

sets.definition: Not bending or yielding; rigid in attitude or position.
sets.example: The judge's unbending rule meant the case was decided in minutes.

sets.definition: Not able to be broken; extremely durable.
sets.example: She bought an unbreakable case for the phone she kept dropping.

sets.clue: The careful ____ turned scraps of wood into a fine chair.
sets.definition: The degree of skill or care shown in making something.
sets.example: The careful workmanship turned scraps of wood into a fine chair.

sets.clue: The act of making something plain by giving more detail.
sets.definition: The act of making something clearer or easier to understand.
sets.example: The diplomat requested clarification on the wording of the treaty.

sets.definition: Unwilling to change or compromise; rigid.
sets.example: The bureaucracy proved inflexible, refusing to make even one small exception.

sets.definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action.
sets.example: Despite hours of pleading, the obdurate landlord refused to lower the rent.

sets.clue: The mayor's public ____ of the new park's opening drew a crowd of hundreds.
sets.definition: An official public announcement.
sets.example: The mayor's public proclamation of the new park's opening drew a crowd of hundreds.

sets.definition: Obedient or attentive to an excessive or servile degree.
sets.example: The courtiers were obsequious in a manner that embarrassed even the king.

sets.clue: Wrongdoing, especially by a public official or in a professional capacity
sets.definition: Wrongdoing, especially by a public official or in a professional capacity.
sets.example: The inquiry uncovered widespread malfeasance in the awarding of contracts.

sets.definition: Not showing due respect for another person; insolent; shamelessly bold.
sets.example: The student's impudent reply silenced the entire classroom.

sets.clue: He continued to _____, offering polite answers that revealed nothing of his true intent.
sets.definition: To conceal one's true motives, feelings, or beliefs; to speak or act hypocritically.
sets.etymology: Old French dis- (away) + sembler (to seem, resemble) — 'to appear different from what one is'
sets.example: He continued to dissemble, offering polite answers that revealed nothing of his true intent.
sets.antonyms: reveal, disclose, be candid, be forthright

sets.clue: Going beyond what is fair or right; exceeding one's authority.
sets.definition: Going beyond what is appropriate, sensible, or allowed.
sets.example: The principal's overreaching new dress code drew protest from parents and students alike.

sets.definition: Showing a lack of respect or courtesy.
sets.example: His disrespectful tone during the meeting cost him the promotion.

sets.definition: Not loyal or trustworthy; failing to keep one's word.
sets.example: The unfaithful steward sold his master's secrets for a bag of gold.

sets.clue: The peasants groaned under the king's ____, which left them with nothing.
sets.definition: Concern only for one's own well-being, without regard for others.
sets.example: The peasants groaned under the king's selfishness, which left them with nothing.

sets.clue: Dishonest or fraudulent conduct, especially by those in power.
sets.definition: Dishonest or fraudulent conduct by those in power, typically for personal gain.
sets.example: A long investigation finally exposed the corruption at the heart of city hall.

sets.clue: excessively eager to please, fawning, servile
sets.definition: Prepared to obey others unquestioningly; excessively willing to serve.
sets.example: The minister grew weary of the subservient aides who agreed with his every word.

sets.definition: Attempting to appear more important or impressive than one really is.
sets.example: Her pretentious use of obscure French phrases impressed no one at the table.

sets.clue: The aide's ____ nods to every comment annoyed everyone else in the room.
sets.definition: Showing excessive respect or submission to authority.
sets.example: The aide's deferential nods to every comment annoyed everyone else in the room.

sets.clue: Having very little or no money; penniless
sets.definition: Having very little or no money; penniless.
sets.etymology: Latin im- (not) + pecunia (money, from pecus, cattle — cattle being early currency) + -ious
sets.example: The once-wealthy merchant died impecunious, leaving nothing to his heirs.

sets.definition: Achieving little or nothing; ineffective; producing no result.
sets.etymology: Old English un- (not) + Old French valoir (to be worth) → avail + -ing
sets.example: All her protests proved unavailing against the committee's decision.

sets.definition: Not having enough of a specified quality or ingredient; lacking.
sets.etymology: Latin de- (down from, away) + facere (to do, make) → deficere (to fail) + -ent
sets.example: The soil was deficient in the minerals needed to sustain the crop.

sets.definition: The feeling of being upset or annoyed as a result of being unable to change or achieve something.
sets.example: The engineer expressed her frustration with the repeated system failures.

sets.clue: The state of having no home.
sets.definition: The condition of having no place to live.
sets.example: The new shelter was built to ease homelessness in the harshest winter months.

sets.clue: missing, gone, not to be found
sets.definition: Not able to be obtained, used, or reached.
sets.example: The book she needed was unavailable at every library in town.

sets.definition: Not yet completed; left incomplete.
sets.example: She left the painting unfinished when she ran out of canvas.

sets.clue: After weeks of looking, the search seemed ____.
sets.definition: Too great in amount or intensity to manage or resist.
sets.example: After weeks of looking, the search seemed overwhelming.

sets.clue: The state of being without something basic that one needs.
sets.definition: The lack or denial of something considered a necessity.
sets.example: Months of sleep deprivation left her foggy and irritable.

sets.definition: Extremely poor; lacking the basic means of subsistence.
sets.example: The war left thousands of families destitute and without shelter.

sets.definition: Not enough to meet a need; lacking in quantity.
sets.example: The harvest was insufficient to feed the village through the winter.

sets.clue: The drought caused great ____ in the village.
sets.definition: Lack of proper nutrition due to insufficient or improper food.
sets.example: The drought caused great malnutrition in the village.

sets.clue: Wary and unwilling to take risks; careful and deliberate
sets.definition: Wary and unwilling to take risks; carefully considering all circumstances.
sets.etymology: Latin circum- (around) + specere (to look) + -spect — 'looking around on all sides'
sets.example: A circumspect investor, she refused to commit until all risks were understood.

sets.definition: In a manner that shows an ability to accurately assess situations and turn them to one's advantage.
sets.example: She astutely observed that the contract contained a damaging clause.

sets.clue: He gave an _____ account of events, full of vivid detail and telling observation.
sets.definition: Intelligently analytical and clear-thinking; having the quality of cutting to the heart of a matter.
sets.example: He gave an incisive account of events, full of vivid detail and telling observation.

sets.definition: Clearly expressed and easily understood; readily apparent.
sets.etymology: Latin per- (through) + specere (to look) + -uous — 'able to be seen through clearly'
sets.example: Her argument was so perspicuous that even the most skeptical listener was persuaded.

sets.clue: The quality of thinking before acting; being mindful of risks.
sets.definition: The quality of giving close attention; deliberate caution.
sets.example: The chemist's carefulness with the bunsen burner kept the lab incident-free for years.

sets.definition: Thinking sensibly; not confused or misled.
sets.example: Even in the panic of the storm, the captain stayed clearheaded and guided the ship to safety.

sets.definition: Showing a clear and deep understanding of a person or situation.
sets.example: Her insightful comment in the meeting changed how everyone saw the problem.

sets.clue: His ____ answers in the interview won him the job.
sets.definition: Possessing or showing a deep understanding of a subject.
sets.example: His knowledgeable answers in the interview won him the job.

sets.clue: The ability to judge well or perceive subtle differences.
sets.definition: Good judgment; the ability to perceive things clearly or wisely.
sets.example: The art collector's discernment saved her from many overpriced fakes.

sets.definition: Having or showing keen mental perception and understanding.
sets.example: A perspicacious reader noticed the inconsistency in the alibi on the second page.

sets.definition: Clarity of thought or expression; the quality of being easily understood.
sets.example: After his long illness, the patient's brief moments of lucidity were a comfort to the family.

sets.clue: Her ____ of the evidence revealed a pattern no one else had seen.
sets.definition: Detailed examination of the elements or structure of something.
sets.example: Her analysis of the evidence revealed a pattern no one else had seen.

sets.clue: Having a long-established and unlikely-to-change pattern of behavior
sets.definition: Having a particular habit, activity, or interest that is long-established and unlikely to change.
sets.example: An inveterate traveler, she had visited forty countries before turning thirty.

sets.definition: Old-fashioned or outdated; belonging to or characteristic of an earlier period.
sets.example: The office ran on an antiqued system that newer staff found baffling.

sets.definition: Lasting for a very short time; transitory.
sets.example: Fame in the age of social media can be frustratingly ephemeral.

sets.clue: The treaty's terms were _____ — valid only so long as both parties saw benefit in them.
sets.definition: Arranged or existing for the present, possibly to be changed later; temporary.
sets.example: The treaty's terms were provisional — valid only so long as both parties saw benefit.

sets.clue: The act of making something new again.
sets.definition: The act of resuming or making something new or strong again.
sets.example: The library's renewal of its membership program drew in dozens of new readers.

sets.definition: No longer in use or current; out of fashion.
sets.example: Her outdated textbook still described the planet count as nine.

sets.definition: Acting without haste; calm and unhurried.
sets.example: She walked with an unhurried pace, stopping to admire every shop window.

sets.clue: The ____ seasons of the year remind us that nothing stays the same.
sets.definition: Constantly changing; never staying the same.
sets.example: The everchanging seasons of the year remind us that nothing stays the same.

sets.clue: The quality of being changeable or inconstant.
sets.definition: The quality of being subject to change.
sets.example: The mutability of fashion makes it costly to dress for trends.

sets.definition: Lasting for an indefinitely long time; enduring.
sets.example: The senator's perennial promise to lower taxes resurfaced every campaign season.

sets.definition: Lasting for only a very short time.
sets.example: A momentary hesitation cost the runner the gold medal.

sets.clue: The ____ reforms transformed every school in the district.
sets.definition: Favoring or showing improvement or change; advancing.
sets.example: The progressive reforms transformed every school in the district.

sets.clue: Using many words where few would do; long-winded
sets.definition: The use of more words than are needed; the quality of being long-winded.
sets.example: The report was admired for its clarity, a welcome change from the committee's usual verbosity.

sets.definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing.
sets.etymology: Latin e- (out) + loqui (to speak) + -ent — 'speaking out' with force and clarity
sets.example: The barrister delivered an eloquent closing argument that moved several jurors to tears.

sets.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; deliberately ambiguous or unclear.
sets.example: His equivocal reply left reporters unsure whether he would resign.

sets.definition: Called into question or challenged the truth, validity, or honesty of.
sets.example: The barrister impugned the witness's credibility with a series of sharp questions.

sets.clue: The act of saying or expressing something again.
sets.definition: The act of expressing something a second time, usually for clarity.
sets.example: The professor's restatement of the rule finally made the students understand it.

sets.definition: Tending to talk too loudly or too much, often without thought.
sets.example: His loudmouthed uncle dominated every family dinner with stories no one wanted to hear.

sets.definition: The state of being completely without sound.
sets.example: The soundlessness of the snowy field made her own footsteps feel loud.

sets.clue: Her ____ answer made it clear she didn't want to discuss it.
sets.definition: Not using words; communicated through gesture or expression.
sets.example: Her nonverbal answer made it clear she didn't want to discuss it.

sets.clue: Unnecessary repetition or excess of words.
sets.definition: Wordy excess; the use of more words than necessary.
sets.example: The editor circled every redundancy in the manuscript with a red pen.

sets.definition: The quality of expressing much in few words.
sets.example: She admired the poet's conciseness — every word carried weight.

sets.definition: Reluctant to speak about one's thoughts or feelings.
sets.example: The normally chatty witness grew reticent when asked about the night of the crime.

sets.clue: The witness's ____ answers gave the jury no clear picture.
sets.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; unclear or vague.
sets.example: The witness's ambiguous answers gave the jury no clear picture.

sets.definition: Shining brilliantly; sending forth radiance.
sets.example: An effulgent dawn broke over the harbor, gilding every rooftop.

sets.definition: To render obscure, unclear, or unintelligible.
sets.example: Lawyers can obfuscate a simple matter until no one knows what was agreed.

sets.definition: To represent in outline; to foreshadow or hint at what is coming.
sets.example: The opening chapter adumbrates themes the rest of the novel will deepen.

sets.clue: Held up to the lamp, the manuscript proved as _____ as window glass.
sets.definition: Translucently clear; lucid and easy to understand.
sets.example: Held up to the lamp, the manuscript proved as pellucid as window glass.

sets.clue: The act of making something brighter.
sets.definition: The act or process of making something less dark.
sets.example: A slow lightening in the eastern sky meant dawn was near.

sets.definition: Made less visible or important by something larger or brighter.
sets.example: The small candle was overshadowed by the bright chandelier above it.

sets.definition: Not blocked by clouds; clear and bright.
sets.example: The hikers reached the summit under an unclouded blue sky.

sets.clue: The ____ garden bloomed under the cloudless sky.
sets.definition: Soaked or saturated with bright sunlight.
sets.example: The sundrenched garden bloomed under the cloudless sky.

sets.clue: To brighten with light or make clear.
sets.definition: To light up; to make something visible or clear.
sets.example: Tall lamps were installed to illuminate the path through the garden after dark.

sets.definition: Shining brilliantly; full of splendor.
sets.example: The queen appeared at the ball resplendent in a gown of woven gold.

sets.definition: Cloudy, hazy, or vague; lacking definite form.
sets.example: His nebulous plan for the company impressed nobody on the board.

sets.clue: The detective's ____ on the case impressed the jury.
sets.definition: Careful thought or consideration; also, the throwing back of light or an image.
sets.example: The detective's reflection on the case impressed the jury.

sets.clue: To speak or act evasively so as to mislead; to dodge the truth
sets.definition: To speak or act evasively so as to mislead; to dodge the truth.
sets.example: Pressed for a clear answer, the minister continued to prevaricate.

sets.definition: Innocently open; lacking craft or guile.
sets.etymology: Latin in- (in, native) + gen- (to be born) + -uus — originally 'freeborn,' later 'frank, noble in spirit'
sets.example: Her ingenuous question disarmed the witness more than any objection had.

sets.definition: Unwilling or unable to believe; skeptical.
sets.example: She listened to the explanation with an incredulous half-smile.

sets.clue: She had a reputation for being relentlessly _____, even when discretion would have served her better.
sets.definition: Habitually truthful; speaking what one believes to be true.
sets.example: She had a reputation for being relentlessly veracious, even when discretion would have served her better.

sets.clue: The quality of being true and honest in what one says.
sets.definition: The quality of telling the truth; honesty in speech.
sets.example: His truthfulness on the witness stand left the jury with no doubts.

sets.definition: Refusing to accept that something is true.
sets.example: She gave him a disbelieving stare when he claimed he had run the marathon.

sets.definition: Not able to be relied on as honest or truthful.
sets.example: After he leaked the secret, his coworkers found him untrustworthy.

sets.clue: Her ____ claims made the lawyers suspicious.
sets.definition: Exaggerated; presented as greater than the reality.
sets.example: Her overstated claims made the lawyers suspicious.

sets.clue: The act of deliberately misleading someone.
sets.definition: The act of causing someone to accept as true what is false.
sets.example: The magician's deception fooled even those who had watched the trick a dozen times.

sets.definition: Not easily convinced; having doubts or reservations.
sets.example: She remained skeptical of the politician's promises until she saw real results.

sets.definition: Not telling the truth; lying.
sets.example: The mendacious account given by the suspect fell apart under cross-examination.

sets.clue: Her ____ raised serious questions about the company's reports.
sets.definition: A difference between two things that should be the same.
sets.example: Her discrepancy raised serious questions about the company's reports.

sets.definition: Showing great care, persistence, and effort.
sets.etymology: Latin ad- (to, at) + sedere (to sit) + -uous — literally 'sitting close to' the task
sets.example: Years of assiduous practice had given her hands a quiet authority.

sets.clue: Habitually averse to exertion; lazy in disposition
sets.definition: Habitually averse to exertion; lazy in disposition.
sets.example: An indolent afternoon stretched out, undisturbed by any thought of work.

sets.definition: Steady persistence in a course of action despite difficulty.
sets.example: It was sheer perseverance, not talent, that finished the manuscript.

sets.clue: He gave the report a _____ glance and signed without comment.
sets.definition: Carried out with a minimum of effort or interest; routine and superficial.
sets.example: He gave the report a perfunctory glance and signed without comment.

sets.clue: The quality of never getting exhausted; great energy.
sets.definition: The quality of being able to work or continue without becoming tired.
sets.example: The volunteer's tirelessness kept the shelter running through three consecutive storms.

sets.definition: Putting in great effort and dedication to one's work.
sets.example: Her hardworking reputation earned her the chance to lead the new project.

sets.definition: A state of lacking energy or enthusiasm.
sets.example: A week of fever left her in a state of listlessness that worried her parents.

sets.clue: His ____ approach to chores left every job partially-done.
sets.definition: Done without enthusiasm or commitment.
sets.example: His halfhearted approach to chores left every job half-done.

sets.clue: The quality of holding firm despite obstacles.
sets.definition: The quality of being determined and persistent.
sets.example: The mountaineer's tenacity brought her to the summit despite the brutal winds.

sets.definition: Hardworking, dedicated, and persistent in effort.
sets.example: The industrious beavers reshaped the entire stream within a single season.

sets.definition: Showing or feeling no interest, enthusiasm, or concern.
sets.example: The teacher worried that her once-eager students had grown apathetic about science.

sets.clue: She pledged her ____ to the cause and never wavered, even when others walked away.
sets.definition: Commitment to a task or purpose.
sets.example: The author's dedication brought the long book to a close after years of work.

sets.clue: Just beginning to develop or come into existence
sets.definition: Just beginning to develop or come into existence.
sets.example: She caught the incipient tremor in his voice before he turned away.

sets.clue: She sensed a ____ fear — not learned but inherited from something ancient in the blood.
sets.definition: Existing from the very beginning; original or primal.
sets.example: The cathedral was built upon a primordial stone the villagers called the World-Anchor.

sets.definition: Marking the formal beginning of an institution, office, or undertaking.
sets.etymology: Latin in- (in, on) + augur (one who reads omens) + -al — because ceremonies were opened with augury
sets.example: Her inaugural address quoted no one and named no one, which surprised the press.

sets.definition: Belonging to a place from the earliest known times; native to a region.
sets.example: The valley's aboriginal name was lost in three centuries of mistranslation.

sets.clue: The act of beginning again after a stop.
sets.definition: The act of starting something again after stopping.
sets.example: After the power outage, the long restarting of every computer in the lab took an hour.

sets.definition: The beginning or establishment of something.
sets.example: From its inception as a small café to a landmark restaurant, the place had always felt welcoming.

sets.definition: The act of being born again or starting fresh.
sets.example: The festival celebrated the rebirthing of the forest after the long winter.

sets.clue: Her ____ years in the village shaped who she became.
sets.definition: Serving to form or shape; relating to an early, decisive stage of development.
sets.example: Her formative years in the village shaped who she became.

sets.clue: The very first stages of something.
sets.definition: The act of beginning or being formally accepted into something new.
sets.example: The new recruits arrived early for the formal initiation into the brotherhood.

sets.definition: Belonging to the earliest ages of the world; ancient.
sets.example: The hikers felt as if they had stepped into a primeval forest untouched by humans.

sets.definition: Belonging to or occurring in the present time.
sets.example: The museum's east wing displays contemporary art from artists still working today.

sets.clue: The ____ chapters of the book set up the entire story.
sets.definition: Coming before something more important; introductory.
sets.example: The preliminary chapters of the book set up the entire story.

sets.clue: She had been known to ____ across three countries on little more than a train pass and a worn notebook.
sets.definition: To travel from place to place, especially on foot or as a pilgrim.
sets.etymology: Latin per (through) + ager (field, land) + -ate — 'to travel through foreign lands'
sets.example: He would peregrinate from coast to coast for months, sending only postcards.

sets.clue: One who wanders without steady employment or fixed home; a rootless drifter.
sets.definition: A person who moves from place to place without a fixed home or settled work.
sets.etymology: Latin vagari (to wander) + -bundus (suffix forming adjectives of inclination)
sets.example: The vagabond slept in barns and paid his way with songs.

sets.definition: Traveling from place to place, especially to work or perform.
sets.example: An itinerant bookseller passed through every spring with the same battered cart.

sets.definition: Not following any regular course; unpredictable in movement or behavior.
sets.example: His erratic attendance made it impossible to plan the rehearsals.

sets.clue: Traveling on foot while carrying one's gear.
sets.definition: The activity of hiking and camping while carrying gear on one's back.
sets.example: She spent the summer backpacking through the national parks of the western states.

sets.definition: Without a guide or clear direction.
sets.example: The unguided hikers spent two extra hours finding their way back to the trailhead.

sets.definition: Not moving; remaining in one place.
sets.example: The deer stood unmoving in the clearing, sensing the hunter's approach.

sets.clue: The ____ in the corner had stories from every continent.
sets.definition: A person who travels frequently to many parts of the world.
sets.example: The globetrotter in the corner had stories from every continent.

sets.clue: The act of traveling for pleasure or learning.
sets.definition: A short journey or trip, especially for pleasure or learning.
sets.example: The biology class spent the day on an excursion to the tidal pools.

sets.definition: Lasting only a short time; passing through without settling.
sets.example: The hotel was filled with transient guests, none staying more than a night.

sets.definition: Moving from one place to another at regular intervals.
sets.example: The migratory birds passed through the valley every October on their way south.

sets.clue: Her years as a ____ shaped her view of the world.
sets.definition: A person who lives outside their native country.
sets.example: Her years as an expatriate shaped her view of the world.

sets.definition: Noble in spirit; generous in forgiving or in judgment of others.
sets.example: It was magnanimous of her to credit the rival who had once tried to silence her.

sets.definition: Marked by great generosity; lavishly giving.
sets.example: A munificent bequest funded the new wing of the library.

sets.definition: Aggressively grasping; given to plunder or excessive acquisition.
sets.example: Their rapacious appetite for new contracts left smaller firms with nothing.

sets.clue: He spent his days cataloguing every coin, an ____ man who kept the house unheated to save on fuel.
sets.definition: Having an insatiable desire to acquire money or possessions; excessively covetous.
sets.example: He spent his days cataloguing every coin, an avaricious man who kept the house unheated to save on fuel.

sets.clue: Free with money or time; giving without holding back.
sets.definition: Giving freely and without reservation; generous.
sets.example: The openhanded host refilled every plate before anyone had to ask.

sets.definition: Unwilling to give or spend money; mean.
sets.example: The tightfisted uncle refused to chip in even a dollar for the family gift.

sets.definition: Not providing assistance or support.
sets.example: The clerk's unhelpful shrug sent the lost tourist looking for someone else.

sets.clue: His ____ gift made the whole family weep with joy.
sets.definition: More than enough; abundant and spilling over.
sets.example: His overflowing gift made the whole family weep with joy.

sets.clue: Driven by a desire to gather and own; inclined to accumulate beyond need.
sets.definition: Tending to acquire things; motivated by the desire for gain and possession.
sets.example: Her acquisitive nature had filled every shelf and drawer before the movers arrived.

sets.definition: Wanting or devouring great quantities of something.
sets.example: The voracious reader finished three novels in a single weekend.

sets.definition: Generous in giving help or doing good for others.
sets.example: The beneficent patron funded scholarships for a generation of artists.

sets.clue: The library's expansion was funded by a single ____ donor.
sets.definition: Seeking to promote the welfare of others, especially by giving money to good causes.
sets.example: The library's expansion was funded by a single philanthropic donor.

sets.clue: Calling something past to mind; evocative of an earlier time
sets.definition: Calling something past to mind; evocative of an earlier time.
sets.example: The kitchen smelled reminiscent of her grandmother's autumn preserves.

sets.definition: Wholly unaware or unmindful of what is happening around one.
sets.etymology: Latin ob- (over) + livere (to be bluish, to be wiped smooth) — in Latin oblivisci, 'to forget' + -ious
sets.example: Headphones on, she was oblivious to the argument three feet behind her.

sets.definition: Designed to aid memory; pertaining to memory.
sets.example: Every medical student knows the gory mnemonic for the cranial nerves.

sets.clue: Each November they gather to _____ the regiment lost at the river.
sets.definition: To honor or recall by ceremony; to keep alive in collective memory.
sets.example: Each November they gather to commemorate the regiment lost at the river.

sets.clue: The act of bringing something back to mind.
sets.definition: The action of remembering or being reminded of something.
sets.example: The veteran's recalling of the old battle drew a hush from the audience.

sets.definition: Not aware of or paying attention to something.
sets.example: The unmindful hiker left her water bottle at every rest stop.

sets.definition: A sentimental longing for the past; pleasure mixed with sadness when remembering earlier times.
sets.example: Whenever she heard that song, a wave of nostalgia swept over her.

sets.clue: His gift for ____ could turn even a dull trip to market into an hour-long adventure.
sets.definition: The activity of telling or sharing stories.
sets.example: Her storytelling of the old neighborhood made everyone laugh and cry.

sets.clue: The act of calling something to mind.
sets.definition: The act or process of remembering something.
sets.example: His recollection of the accident matched the witness's testimony exactly.

sets.definition: Unable to be removed, erased, or forgotten.
sets.example: The artist signed every canvas with indelible ink.

sets.definition: Not worth remembering; lacking distinction.
sets.example: The film was so unmemorable that no one could recall the plot a week later.

sets.clue: The ceremony was held to ____ the founders of the school.
sets.definition: To regard with great respect or reverence.
sets.example: The ceremony was held to venerate the founders of the school.

sets.clue: The two colleagues were so _____ to each other that their manager had to assign them to separate teams.
sets.definition: Having or showing a strong aversion or repugnance
Friendly and good-natured in tone; characterized by goodwill.
sets.example: The two firms reached an amicable settlement before any letter reached the press.

sets.clue: Eager to fight or quarrel; warlike in temperament
sets.definition: Eager to fight or quarrel; warlike in temperament.
sets.example: After two whiskeys he grew strangely bellicose about a chess match from last spring.

sets.definition: Tending to obstruct or harm; hostile in effect, if not always in intent.
sets.example: Cold drafts are inimical to the violins, even more than to the players.

sets.clue: The quality of being simple and welcoming.
sets.definition: A warm, simple, and welcoming quality, like a cozy home.
sets.example: The inn's homeliness made travelers feel like family from the moment they arrived.

sets.definition: Kind, generous, and willing to give freely.
sets.example: The bighearted coach paid for the new uniforms out of his own pocket.

sets.definition: Not friendly; cold or hostile in manner.
sets.example: The new neighbors were unfriendly for the first month, then warmed up to the block.

sets.clue: She was not rude, merely ____, and guests learned not to expect warmth or conversation from her corner of the room.
sets.definition: Distant and cold in manner; aloof.
sets.example: The standoffish stranger refused even a glass of water from the locals.

sets.definition: The quality of being lively, friendly, and enjoyable in company.
sets.example: The conviviality of the dinner party kept everyone at the table until midnight.

sets.definition: To render fit, suitable, or correspondent; to adapt.
sets.example: to accommodate ourselves to circumstances
sets.synonyms: adapt, adjust, arrange, conform, suit, reconcile, oblige

sets.clue: His _____ style made every meeting tense and unproductive.
sets.definition: Tending toward confrontation; aggressive in dispute.
sets.example: His confrontational style made every meeting tense and unproductive.

sets.clue: Well-meaning and kindly disposed toward others.
sets.definition: Well-meaning and kindly disposed toward others.
sets.example: The host's benevolent manner put every guest immediately at ease.

sets.clue: Deserving or causing a feeling of shame or embarrassment.
sets.definition: Deserving or causing a feeling of shame or embarrassment.
sets.example: It was shameful that no one stopped to help the injured animal by the road.

sets.definition: Showing excessive pride in one's achievements or possessions.
sets.example: His boastful talk about his exam scores made the rest of the class uncomfortable.

sets.definition: Feeling no guilt or embarrassment about something others might consider wrong.
sets.example: She was completely unashamed of her unconventional hobby and spoke about it freely.

sets.clue: She was ____ when the teacher read her private poem aloud to the whole class.
sets.definition: Showing redness of the face from embarrassment or shame.
sets.example: She was blushing when the teacher read her private poem aloud to the whole class.

sets.clue: Excessive pride in one's appearance, abilities, or achievements; an inflated, self-admiring regard.
sets.definition: Excessive pride in one's own appearance, abilities, or achievements.
sets.example: His vanity was such that he paused at every reflective surface he passed.

sets.definition: Having an exaggerated sense of one's own importance or abilities.
sets.example: The arrogant manager dismissed every team suggestion without a moment's consideration.

sets.definition: Extremely embarrassed or ashamed, especially in a public or social situation.
sets.example: He was mortified when his mother used his childhood nickname in front of his new colleagues.

sets.clue: Full of ____, he tracked down every person he had wronged and apologised in person.
sets.definition: A feeling of deep remorse and regret for wrongdoing.
sets.example: Full of contrition, he tracked down every person he had wronged and apologised in person.

sets.clue: Excessive pride or self-confidence, especially the kind seen as an affront to the gods and an invitation to retribution.
sets.definition: Excessive pride or self-confidence, especially when seen as provoking retributive punishment.
sets.etymology: Greek hybris (wanton insolence, violence) — denoting defiant transgression of divine limits
sets.example: His hubris in assuming he could outmanoeuvre the whole market led to spectacular losses.

sets.definition: Excessive pride in one's own achievements, typically expressed through empty boasting.
sets.example: The general's speech was marred by its vainglory — every sentence circled back to his own heroism.

sets.definition: Public shame and loss of honour; great disgrace.
sets.example: Caught cheating, the athlete faced the ignominy of a public hearing and a stripped medal.

sets.clue: Despite winning the argument, she felt _____ when she saw how deeply her words had wounded him.
sets.definition: Feeling or expressing genuine remorse and regret for wrongdoing.
sets.example: Despite winning the argument, she felt contrite when she saw how deeply her words had wounded him.

sets.clue: Feeling or showing extreme fear, especially as a sudden reaction.
sets.definition: Feeling or showing extreme fear, especially as a sudden reaction.
sets.example: The frightened puppy hid under the bed during the thunderstorm.

sets.definition: Not deterred by danger or pain; showing courage.
sets.example: It was courageous of her to speak up about the bullying when everyone else stayed quiet.

sets.definition: Made anxious or agitated; having lost confidence or composure.
sets.example: The unexpected question left him completely unnerved, and he forgot everything he had rehearsed.

sets.clue: The ____ of the rescue worker, who climbed into the unstable tunnel without hesitating, was remarkable.
sets.definition: The quality of being daring and willing to face danger with confidence.
sets.example: The boldness of the rescue worker, who climbed into the unstable tunnel without hesitating, was remarkable.

sets.clue: Anxious or uneasy about a possible future event or uncertain outcome.
sets.definition: Anxious or uneasy about a possible future event.
sets.example: She felt apprehensive about starting a new school where she knew nobody.

sets.definition: Possessing or showing courage, especially in the face of hardship.
sets.example: The valiant firefighters worked through the night to contain the blaze.

sets.definition: Great fear or apprehension, especially about something that is to come.
sets.etymology: Old English drǣdan (to fear greatly) — related to Gothic us-þriutan (to oppress)
sets.example: She faced the exam with a mixture of dread and grim determination.

sets.clue: His ____ in confronting the entire board alone, without a single ally in the room, was striking.
sets.definition: The willingness to take bold risks; confident, sometimes reckless daring.
sets.example: His audacity in confronting the entire board alone, without a single ally in the room, was striking.

sets.clue: Showing a shameful lack of courage; contemptibly cowardly.
sets.definition: Showing a contemptible lack of courage; cowardly.
sets.example: The pusillanimous official refused to sign the report, citing vague personal concerns.

sets.definition: Fearless and adventurous; resolutely bold.
sets.example: The intrepid correspondent filed dispatches from five conflict zones that year.

sets.definition: A feeling of fear or agitation about something that may happen; trembling anxiety.
sets.example: She approached the microphone with some trepidation, having never addressed so large a crowd.

sets.clue: It was her _____ that kept her training through injury, loss, and two failed attempts.
sets.definition: Courage and resilience in enduring pain or adversity without complaint.
sets.example: It was her fortitude that kept her training through injury, loss, and two failed attempts.

sets.clue: Lacking good sense or judgment; unwise.
sets.definition: Lacking good sense or judgment; unwise.
sets.example: It was foolish to leave the house without an umbrella when the clouds were so dark.

sets.definition: Showing careful consideration; absorbed in thought.
sets.example: The thoughtful student read the question twice before picking up her pen.

sets.definition: Not giving enough attention or thought to what one does.
sets.example: A careless reading of the instructions caused him to assemble the whole shelf backwards.

sets.clue: The ____ professor could speak knowledgeably on any subject from astronomy to zoology.
sets.definition: Having acquired much knowledge through study and experience.
sets.example: The learned professor could speak knowledgeably on any subject from astronomy to zoology.

sets.clue: Wisdom and sound judgment, especially in practical and everyday matters.
sets.definition: Wisdom and sound judgment, especially in practical matters.
sets.example: The village elder's sagacity was sought by neighbours from miles around.

sets.definition: Acting or done quickly without thought or care; rashly impulsive.
sets.example: Her impetuous decision to quit without a plan left her regretting it for months.

sets.definition: Wildly unreasonable, illogical, or inappropriate.
sets.example: The committee found the proposal so absurd that several members laughed aloud.

sets.clue: It was ____ of her to save three months of expenses before handing in her notice.
sets.definition: Acting with care and thought for the future; showing good judgment.
sets.example: It was prudent of her to save three months of expenses before handing in her notice.

sets.clue: Having a ready insight into things; shrewd and discerning in perception and judgment.
sets.definition: Having a ready insight into things; shrewd and discerning.
sets.example: The perspicacious reviewer spotted the structural flaw that had eluded three editors.

sets.definition: Possessing great wisdom and sound judgment.
sets.example: The sapient counsellor had guided three generations of the family through their disputes.

sets.definition: Pointless foolishness; stupid or senseless behaviour.
sets.example: The plan was dismissed as sheer fatuity — expensive, unworkable, and based on no evidence.

sets.clue: The senator dismissed the amendment as _____, saying it belonged in a playground, not a legislature.
sets.definition: Childishly silly and trivial; immature.
sets.example: The senator dismissed the amendment as puerile, saying it belonged in a playground, not a legislature.

sets.clue: Having a great amount of money or valuable possessions; rich.
sets.definition: Having a great amount of money or valuable possessions; rich.
sets.example: The wealthy family donated a new wing to the local library.

sets.definition: Careful and economical in the use of money and resources.
sets.example: Her thrifty habits meant she had enough savings to travel every summer.

sets.definition: Having no money at all; utterly without funds.
sets.example: He arrived in the city penniless, with nothing but a suitcase and a phone number.

sets.clue: It seemed ____ to throw away half the banquet food just because the event was over.
sets.definition: Using or expending something carelessly or in excess; extravagant.
sets.example: It seemed wasteful to throw away half the banquet food just because the event was over.

sets.clue: Without the basic necessities of life; extremely poor.
sets.definition: Without the basic necessities of life; extremely poor.
sets.example: The earthquake left thousands of families destitute, without shelter or clean water.

sets.definition: Using or giving in generous, abundant, or extravagant amounts.
sets.example: The lavish ceremony cost more than the couple earned in a year.

sets.definition: The quality of being careful and economical with resources; avoidance of waste.
sets.example: Years of frugality allowed her to retire a full decade earlier than her peers.

sets.clue: Coming from an ____ family, she had attended the finest schools and travelled every summer.
sets.definition: Having a great deal of money; wealthy.
sets.example: Coming from an affluent family, she had attended the finest schools and travelled every summer.

sets.clue: Extreme poverty; the state of having insufficient money to live on.
sets.definition: Extreme poverty; the state of having insufficient money to live on.
sets.example: She had grown up in penury, sharing one room with four siblings and surviving on scraps.

sets.definition: Ostentatiously rich and luxurious.
sets.example: The opulent ballroom, lined with gold leaf and crystal chandeliers, stunned every visitor.

sets.definition: Extreme unwillingness to spend money or use resources; stinginess.
sets.example: His parsimony was legendary: he reused tea bags and rationed his own heating.

sets.clue: The _____ patron funded an entire hospital wing without seeking recognition or a naming right.
sets.definition: More generous than is usual or necessary; lavishly giving.
sets.example: The munificent patron funded an entire hospital wing without seeking recognition or a naming right.

sets.clue: The quality or state of being clean, tidy, and systematically arranged.
sets.definition: The quality or state of being clean, tidy, and systematically arranged.
sets.example: The inspector praised the neatness of the workroom, where every tool had its own hook.

sets.definition: Untidy or dirty; lacking organisation.
sets.example: After the party, the kitchen was so messy that it took two hours to restore it.

sets.definition: Difficult to control or discipline; not submitting to order.
sets.example: The substitute teacher struggled to manage the unruly class all afternoon.

sets.clue: The new librarian kept everything ____: every book returned to its exact position on the shelf.
sets.definition: Neatly and methodically arranged; well-organised.
sets.example: The new librarian kept everything orderly: every book returned to its exact position on the shelf.

sets.clue: An official system of rules governing formal procedures or conduct.
sets.definition: An official system of rules governing formal procedures or conduct.
sets.etymology: Greek protokollon (first sheet glued to a manuscript) — protos (first) + kolla (glue) + -col
sets.example: Staff were reminded that the safety protocol required two signatures before any valve was opened.

sets.definition: A state of disorder resulting from the absence of authority or governing rules.
sets.example: When the last official fled, the city descended into anarchy for three weeks.

sets.definition: Lacking any plan, order, or direction; random.
sets.etymology: Middle English hap (luck, chance) + Old French hasard (game of dice)
sets.example: The investigation was so haphazard that key witnesses were never interviewed.

sets.clue: The detective was ____: she interviewed every witness twice and catalogued each piece of evidence.
sets.definition: Done according to a careful and orderly plan; systematic.
sets.example: The detective was methodical: she interviewed every witness twice and catalogued each piece of evidence.

sets.clue: Wild and noisy disorder or confusion; a scene of complete uproar.
sets.definition: Wild and noisy disorder or confusion; complete uproar.
sets.etymology: Coined by Milton — Greek pan (all) + daimon (demon) + -ium; originally the capital of Hell
sets.example: Pandemonium broke out in the stands when the referee reversed the final-second call.

sets.definition: The tendency of a system to move toward disorder and degradation over time.
sets.example: Left unmaintained, the garden showed the slow entropy that claims any structure without care.

sets.definition: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise.
sets.example: The restorer's meticulous work took three years but returned the painting to its original state.

sets.clue: The elders gathered to _____ the community's unwritten customs into a formal charter.
sets.definition: To arrange rules, laws, or principles into a systematic code.
sets.example: The elders gathered to codify the community's unwritten customs into a formal charter.

sets.clue: Having or casting a dark, hopeless, or melancholy mood.
sets.definition: Having or casting a dark, hopeless, or melancholy mood.
sets.example: He sat alone by the window, too gloomy to join the celebrations downstairs.

sets.definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic; pleasantly bright.
sets.example: A cheerful greeting from the receptionist put every visitor at ease.

sets.definition: Feeling or expressing intense sorrow, especially over a loss or death.
sets.etymology: Old French grever (to burden, harm) — Latin gravare (to weigh down) + -ing
sets.example: The grieving family sat together through the long night after the funeral.

sets.clue: The ____ kitten batted at the dangling string and rolled across the floor in pursuit.
sets.definition: Fond of games and amusement; light-heartedly fun.
sets.example: The playful kitten batted at the dangling string and rolled across the floor in pursuit.

sets.clue: In low spirits from loss of hope or courage; deeply dejected.
sets.definition: In low spirits from loss of hope or courage; deeply dejected.
sets.etymology: Latin despondere (to give up, lose heart) — de- + spondere (to promise) + -ent
sets.example: After months of rejection letters, she grew despondent and nearly abandoned her novel.

sets.definition: Perfect happiness or great joy.
sets.example: The week on the island, free of all obligations, was pure bliss.

sets.definition: To express passionate grief or regret; to mourn.
sets.example: She continued to lament the demolished building decades after it was torn down.

sets.clue: She was ____ when she opened the letter — she ran straight out to tell her neighbours.
sets.definition: Ecstatically happy; in high spirits.
sets.example: She was elated when she opened the letter — she ran straight out to tell her neighbours.

sets.clue: A slow, mournful piece of music or song composed as a lament for the dead.
sets.definition: A slow, mournful piece of music or song composed as a lament for the dead.
sets.etymology: Latin dirige (direct! — imperative of dirigere), opening word of a funeral psalm
sets.example: The bagpipers played a dirge as the coffin was carried slowly down the hillside.

sets.definition: An intense feeling of happiness and excitement, often overwhelming.
sets.example: The euphoria of the final whistle gave way to tears when the reality sank in.

sets.definition: Having a mournful quality, like that of an elegy; expressing sorrow for the past.
sets.example: The film's closing sequence had an elegiac mood, dwelling on loss and the passage of time.

sets.clue: The translator admired the poem's _____ — every phrase was perfectly fitted to its moment.
sets.definition: Intense happiness; also, the pleasing aptness of a phrase or style.
sets.example: The translator admired the poem's felicity — every phrase was perfectly fitted to its moment.

sets.clue: Made by chemical processes rather than obtained from natural sources; not genuine.
sets.definition: Made by chemical synthesis rather than found in nature; not genuine.
sets.example: The jacket was filled with synthetic down, which performed well but felt slightly stiff.

sets.definition: Not domesticated or controlled; wild.
sets.example: The untamed marshland was home to dozens of species found nowhere else in the region.

sets.definition: Produced or involving production without artificial chemicals; relating to nature.
sets.example: She switched to an organic diet after learning how much pesticide remained on shop produce.

sets.clue: The jeweller had ____ each ring by hand, bending and soldering with complete precision.
sets.definition: Made with skill and care, especially by hand.
sets.example: The jeweller had crafted each ring by hand, bending and soldering with complete precision.

sets.clue: Relating to or evoking rural life, especially in an idealised way.
sets.definition: Relating to or depicting rural life, especially in a gentle or idealised way.
sets.example: The painter specialised in pastoral scenes of rolling hills, farmhouses, and grazing cattle.

sets.definition: Deliberately created rather than arising naturally; lacking spontaneity.
sets.etymology: Old French controver (to devise, find out) — Latin con- + tropare (to compose) + -ed
sets.example: The film's happy ending felt contrived, as though the screenwriter had forced it into shape.

sets.definition: Invented or falsified; also, produced by manufacturing.
sets.example: The alibi was entirely fabricated; the timestamps on the photos proved he was elsewhere.

sets.clue: The ____ trees had thrived in this valley for centuries before any settlers arrived.
sets.definition: Originating or occurring naturally in a particular place; native.
sets.example: The indigenous trees had thrived in this valley for centuries before any settlers arrived.

sets.clue: An image or representation that resembles the real thing but lacks its true substance or authenticity.
sets.definition: An image or representation of something, especially one that looks real but lacks the substance or qualities of the original.
sets.example: The resort was a glossy simulacrum of paradise — pristine on the surface, hollow beneath.
sets.antonyms: original, archetype, prototype, the genuine article

sets.definition: In its original condition; unspoiled and pure.
sets.example: The beach had been closed for decades, leaving it pristine — white sand and clear water, untouched.

sets.definition: Of doubtful authenticity, especially of a story or statement claimed to be true.
sets.example: The tale of the founder's dramatic escape is almost certainly apocryphal, invented long after his death.

sets.clue: The delta's _____ soil had fed farming communities for more than ten thousand years.
sets.definition: Producing or capable of producing an abundance of offspring or new growth; fertile.
sets.example: The delta's fecund soil had fed farming communities for more than ten thousand years.

sets.clue: Putting others' needs before your own; not selfish.
sets.definition: Putting others' needs before your own; not selfish.
sets.example: Her unselfish decision to share gave everyone a turn.

sets.definition: The quality of caring about others' feelings or needs.
sets.example: Her thoughtfulness showed in every small gesture.

sets.definition: The quality of being kind and pleasant to others.
sets.example: The town was known for the friendliness of its people.

sets.clue: She gave a ____ reply to every question, no matter how rude.
sets.definition: Showing politeness and consideration for others.
sets.example: She gave a respectful reply to every question, no matter how rude.

sets.clue: The act of keeping a home in order.
sets.definition: The management and care of a household.
sets.example: Daily housekeeping kept the small apartment feeling spacious and calm.

sets.definition: Free from mess or excess; clean and orderly.
sets.example: Her uncluttered desk made it easy to focus on the task at hand.

sets.definition: Put into a new order or arrangement.
sets.example: The rearranged living room felt brand new without a single new purchase.

sets.clue: The room's ____ made it feel twice as large.
sets.definition: The act of removing unnecessary items to create space.
sets.example: The room's decluttering made it feel twice as large.

sets.clue: Not yet taught to behave or follow commands.
sets.definition: Lacking the discipline or skill that comes from training.
sets.example: The untrained puppy bolted out the door the first chance it got.

sets.definition: Set free from restraint; let loose.
sets.example: Once unleashed, the husky sprinted in a wide circle around the field.

sets.definition: A calm, quiet, and gentle state.
sets.example: The old dog's peacefulness made him the favorite of every visiting child.

sets.clue: The ____ puppy chewed every shoe in the house.
sets.definition: Playfully troublesome; causing minor harm in a fun-loving way.
sets.example: The mischievous puppy chewed every shoe in the house.

sets.clue: Foretelling future events; like a prophet.
sets.definition: Accurately predicting or foretelling future events.
sets.example: Her warning about the storm proved prophetic — it struck within the hour.

sets.definition: Having or showing knowledge of events before they take place.
sets.example: His prescient warning saved the expedition from disaster.

sets.definition: Quick to notice or understand things.
sets.example: A perceptive reader will catch the joke on first read.

sets.clue: The ____ general spotted the ambush before his scouts did.
sets.definition: Keeping careful watch for possible danger.
sets.example: The vigilant general spotted the ambush before his scouts did.

sets.clue: The act of speaking words clearly and distinctly.
sets.definition: Clear and distinct pronunciation of words.
sets.example: The actor's careful enunciation carried every word to the back of the theater.

sets.definition: Deep, full, and reverberating in sound.
sets.example: The cellist's resonant low notes filled every corner of the hall.

sets.definition: Expressing oneself in vehement, loud, or forceful words.
sets.example: The vociferous protesters drowned out every speaker the mayor tried to introduce.

sets.clue: The audience's ____ filled the hall after the final note.
sets.definition: Loud and enthusiastic approval, typically by applause or shouting.
sets.example: The audience's acclamation filled the hall after the final note.

sets.clue: The act of driving or pushing something forward.
sets.definition: The action of driving or pushing forward.
sets.example: The rocket's propulsion carried it past the atmosphere in minutes.

sets.definition: Done with speed and efficiency.
sets.example: The team's expeditious response saved the building from total loss.

sets.definition: Done with careful consideration and at an unhurried pace.
sets.example: She gave a slow, deliberate nod and signed the contract without another word.

sets.clue: The train's ____ through the long tunnel was barely noticed.
sets.definition: The process of moving forward gradually.
sets.example: The train's progression through the long tunnel was barely noticed.

sets.clue: Tending toward harmony or peaceful agreement.
sets.definition: Intended to placate or reconcile; meant to win goodwill.
sets.example: Her conciliatory tone defused the standoff in the boardroom.

sets.definition: A state of agreement or conformity.
sets.example: The judgment was rendered in accordance with established precedent.

sets.definition: Free from conflict; pleasingly consistent.
sets.example: The choir produced a harmonious blend few ensembles could match.

sets.clue: After hours of negotiation, the two factions reached a ____ settlement.
sets.definition: Reached by general agreement of those involved.
sets.example: After hours of negotiation, the two factions reached a consensual settlement.

sets.clue: A state of strong disagreement or conflict.
sets.definition: Disagreement that leads to discord, especially within a group.
sets.example: Open dissension among the cabinet members forced the prime minister to call an early vote.

sets.definition: Hostile and aggressive; inclined to fight.
sets.example: The belligerent patron was asked to leave after threatening the bartender.

sets.definition: Showing or causing active hostility or opposition.
sets.example: The committee's antagonistic tone made compromise impossible from the first meeting.

sets.clue: The two factions reached a state of ____ after years of feud.
sets.definition: The restoration of friendly relations.
sets.example: The two factions reached a state of reconciliation after years of feud.

sets.clue: To argue strongly against something; to protest forcefully.
sets.definition: To make a forcefully reproachful protest.
sets.example: The architects publicly remonstrated against the demolition of the historic library.

sets.definition: Able to convince others through argument or appeal.
sets.example: Her persuasive closing remarks swung three undecided votes her way.

sets.definition: Not seeming reasonable or probable; failing to convince.
sets.example: His implausible alibi collapsed under the first question from the prosecutor.

sets.clue: The lawyer's ____ swayed even the most skeptical jurors.
sets.definition: A forceful and impassioned speech.
sets.example: The lawyer's declamation swayed even the most skeptical jurors.
